literal: the doctrine that the effect (*kārya*) is real (*sat*) [in the cause]
invariant_definition: *Satkārya-vāda* is the thesis that the effect does not come from sheer non-being but pre-exists in its cause in some determinable way. The classical opponent is Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika *ārambha-vāda* or *asatkārya-vāda*, according to which production is the genuine beginning of a previously non-existent effect. Sāṅkhya makes the foundational argument: what is wholly absent cannot be produced; production is manifestation, differentiation, or transformation of what was already causally present. Vedāntins broadly inherit that anti-ex-nihilo commitment, but they disagree sharply over how the effect pre-exists. For some the cause really transforms into its effect; for Advaita the effect is only an apparent manifestation on an unchanged substrate; for Vallabha the manifestation is real without entailing corruption of the cause. The doctrine is therefore not one single theory but the causal battlefield on which *pariṇāma*, *vivarta*, and *avikṛta-pariṇāma* are distinguished.

school_framing.shared_core: *Satkārya-vāda* names **the thesis that the effect pre-exists in its material cause prior to manifestation** (*Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 9). Sāṃkhya and Vedāntic schools that accept it (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Tattva-vāda's mode of it, Vallabha, Nimbārka) share the structural claim. Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika defends *asatkārya-vāda*: the effect is a genuinely new substance produced by inherence in a novel substrate.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same M+LD register, same scope-inside ('effect-cause modal structure'), opposing claims (AF1). Advaita's *vivarta-vāda* is **a different concept** built on *satkārya* as starting point but adding *anirvacanīya* sublatability — methodology §4.

per_school.definition: Advaita accepts kāryakāraṇānanyatva: the world has no being apart from Brahman, and the clay-pot illustration is read to show that the effect is not independently real over against its cause. [cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14] Yet Śaṅkara simultaneously treats vikāra as mere nāmadheya and anṛta, so satkārya here culminates not in a real pariṇāma of Brahman but in the dependent appearance of effect upon an unchanged cause. [cite://sankara/brahma-sutra-bhasya/2.1.14]

Viśiṣṭādvaita reads `sad eva` as teaching that the world already exists in Brahman in subtle condition, and the clay example establishes kāryakāraṇānanyatva because the effect is nothing apart from its material cause. [cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§15; cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§16] The effect is nevertheless real and articulated: the cit-acit cosmos is taught as Brahman's own body, so manifestation is a real emergence of modes rather than an illusory appearance or a severance from the cause. [cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6; cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§18]

Tattva-vāda does not admit causal non-difference between Brahman and the world, because the fivefold difference is real, beginningless, and not sublated by liberation. [cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112] Still, dravya, karma, kāla, svabhāva, and jīva subsist only through Hari's anugraha, while abheda-language is restricted to likeness, predominance, or dependence rather than svarūpābheda. [cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.1.13-15; cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69]

In the available corpus, Bhedābheda is recoverable through Rāmānuja's report of positions for which Brahman, when connected with an upādhi, undergoes bondage, release, and manifold impure transformations, or is by nature both different and non-different while serving as the locus of cosmic transformation. [cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§8; cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§9] On that witness, the school treats the world as a real pariṇāma of Brahman and therefore affirms an effect already grounded in its cause without reducing plurality to illusion. [cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§8; cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§9]

Jīva Gosvāmin construes the abheda-texts through Bhagavān's svābhāvika acintya-śakti, so that the jīva-samūha stands to him as ray-atoms to the sun and is therefore neither simply identical nor simply separate. [cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43] Precisely because bheda remains alongside abheda, bhajana of Bhagavān becomes the abhidheya, and ontological dependence is preserved without collapse into bare identity. [cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43; cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44]

Nimbārka construes bheda and abheda as svābhāvika: the jīva is atomic, knowing, transmigratory, and dependent on Hari, so difference and non-difference are both intrinsic to the relation. [cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1] Because the triadic reality of cit, acit, and Brahman is affirmed as `brahmātmakatva` without cancellation of distinction, effectual plurality is a real dependent manifestation rather than an independent second principle. [cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/7; cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42]

Vallabha explicitly makes Brahman the samavāyi cause and places nāma and rūpa on the effect-side as kārya-dharmas. [cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4a; cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.1.4d] The world is thus a real pariṇāma in kāryākāra, yet Brahman remains avikṛta; this is the school's avikṛta-pariṇāma construal of satkārya. [cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26a; cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26b; cite://vallabha/anubhasya/1.4.26c]

Sāṃkhya states satkārya directly: the effect is existent because the non-existent is not produced, a material cause must be taken up, not everything arises from everything, the potent produces only the producible, and effect follows the nature of its cause. [cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/9] Those five reasons make production a manifestation of what the upādāna already holds, not the origination of an entity that was wholly absent. [cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/9]

Yoga frames causal emergence through pariṇāma of dharmin and dharmas, so the effect belongs to an ongoing continuum of transformation rather than to sheer origination from non-being. [cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.13; cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/3.14] Vyāsa then states that past and future dharmas exist in their own form, and that causal conditions make an already-grounded result present instead of producing an unprecedented entity. [cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.12]

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika defines cause as what antecedently exists with reference to the effect and defines the effect as the counterpositive of prior non-existence, so its model is ārambha rather than satkārya. [cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/38; cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/39] Production terminates prāgabhāva and yields a new kārya, even while the causal nexus remains ordered through samavāyi, asamavāyi, and nimitta causes. [cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/80; cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/40]

primary_loci: Jaimini-Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra and available on-disk commentarial corpus inspected
per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the available Jaimini-Śabara-Kumārila corpus, the school's technical concern is ritual efficacy, codanā, and apūrva rather than a distinct satkārya-vāda doctrine.

per_school.definition: Mādhyamaka does not replace satkārya-vāda with another realist account of production, but denies under svabhāva-analysis that entities ever arise from self, other, both, or no cause. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.1] Nāgārjuna further says that causal conditioning is coherent neither for the existent nor for the non-existent, so pre-existent effect and freshly produced effect are alike rejected at the level of ultimate analysis. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/1.6]

Yogācāra redescribes causality as vijñānapariṇāma: the operative transformations are transformations of consciousness itself. [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/1] The ālaya as sarvabījaka and the paratantra nature as pratyayodbhava show that the effect arises through conditioned unfolding of an already-seeded stream rather than through external material origination ex nihilo. [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/18; cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/21]

Kṣemarāja states that autonomous consciousness alone is the cause of the manifestation, maintenance, and reabsorption of the world. [cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/1] He then glosses `unmīlana` as the making-manifest of what is already situated on consciousness's own surface, so causal production is disclosure of a pre-contained universe rather than origination from an external material. [cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/2]

Jain ontology defines the real as endowed with origination, decay, and permanence, and defines substance as possessed of qualities and modes. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/sat-dravya-lakshanam; cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/guna-paryayavat-dravyam] Accordingly, change is `pariṇāma` of an abiding dravya, so effectual becoming is neither sheer annihilation nor origination from what was wholly absent. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/tad-bhavah-parinamah]

per_school.not_applicable_reason: No substantive school treatment of satkārya-vāda is retrievable from the available on-disk grammatical corpus, and the key Vaiyākaraṇa primaries named for such a judgment are not present here.

translator_note: Avoid flattening *satkārya-vāda* to a generic 'conservation' thesis or to Aristotelian potency without comment. Those comparisons help only at a distance. The doctrine concerns how effecthood is already grounded in the cause within Indian debates over manifestation, transformation, and dependence.
